note_data.title: Dynamics
keyPoints: **Force** is any push or pull that can change the motion of an object or cause deformation
Newton's **first law** states that an object will remain at rest or continue moving at constant velocity unless acted upon by a resultant force
Newton's **second law** states that the resultant force is directly proportional to the rate of change of momentum: F = ma when mass is constant
Newton's **third law** states that when object A exerts a force on object B, object B exerts an equal and opposite force on object A
**Momentum** is the product of mass and velocity: p = mv, measured in kg m s⁻¹
**Impulse** equals the change in momentum and also equals force × time: FΔt = Δp
The **principle of conservation of momentum** states that total momentum before collision equals total momentum after collision in the absence of external forces
In **elastic collisions**, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved
In **inelastic collisions**, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not conserved

definitions.definition: A push or pull that can change the motion of an object or cause deformation, measured in Newtons (N)
The product of mass and velocity of an object, measured in kg m s⁻¹
The product of force and time for which it acts, equal to change in momentum, measured in N s
The total momentum of a system remains constant when no external forces act on the system

howToAnswer.instruction: Give Newton's law in one sentence exactly as worded. No explanation needed.
Give the physics principle + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Write out each step of calculation clearly with units at each stage
Substitute values into correct formula, show working, give answer with correct units and significant figures

modelAnswer.question: A cricket ball of mass 0.16 kg moving at 25 m s⁻¹ is caught by a fielder. The ball comes to rest in 0.20 s. Calculate the average force exerted by the fielder's hands.
modelAnswer.answer:
Initial momentum = mv = 0.16 × 25 = 4.0 kg m s⁻¹
Final momentum = 0 kg m s⁻¹
Change in momentum = 0 - 4.0 = -4.0 kg m s⁻¹
Using F = Δp/Δt
F = -4.0/0.20 = -20 N
Magnitude of force = 20 N
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct calculation of initial momentum (4.0 kg m s⁻¹)
Mark 2: Correct use of F = Δp/Δt or impulse equation
Mark 3: Correct final answer with unit (20 N)

commonMistakes: Never say 'F = ma always applies' — only when mass is constant. Use F = Δp/Δt for general case
Students often forget that momentum is a vector — direction matters in collision calculations
Never confuse impulse with momentum — impulse is change in momentum, not momentum itself
Students often forget to consider both objects in collision problems when applying conservation of momentum
Never ignore the negative sign in force calculations — it indicates direction
examinerTips: This topic appears every year in Paper 2, especially collision and impulse calculations
Always define your positive direction clearly in collision problems
For Newton's laws questions, learn the exact wording — paraphrasing loses marks
Impulse questions often involve cricket balls, cars braking, or collisions — familiar Pakistani contexts
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Free body diagrams showing all forces acting on an object with arrows indicating magnitude and direction, essential for applying Newton's laws correctly
